terms.def: Standard HF fee: 2% annual management fee on AUM + 20% performance fee on profits above hurdle rate.
Minimum return before the performance fee applies; often the risk-free rate or LIBOR/SOFR.
Previous peak NAV; performance fees only charged when fund exceeds it — protects investors from paying twice.
Net = longs − shorts (directional risk). Gross = longs + |shorts| (total leverage).
Minimum time investors must keep capital in the fund; allows the manager to hold illiquid positions.
Redemption limits during stress — fund limits withdrawals to a % of NAV per quarter to prevent forced selling.

eli3: Hedge funds are private investment vehicles that use sophisticated strategies — long-short equity, global macro, relative value arbitrage, event-driven — to generate absolute returns uncorrelated with the stock market. Unlike mutual funds, they can short-sell, use leverage, and invest in illiquid or complex instruments. They typically charge '2 and 20': 2% annual management fee and 20% of profits.

The word 'hedge' in hedge fund is historical — original hedge funds hedged long equity positions with short sales. Today, many hedge funds take concentrated directional bets and don't hedge at all. The distinguishing feature is the legal structure (limited partnership, accredited investors only, limited regulation) and the fee model, not the hedging itself.

Hedge fund performance is highly dispersed: the top quartile generates genuine alpha; the bottom quartile destroys wealth after fees. Due diligence on hedge funds requires evaluating the manager's edge (what information or process do they have that others don't?), risk management framework, and fee structure. High water marks protect investors from paying fees twice on recovered losses.

whenWhy.title: Hedge fund strategies overview
whenWhy.body: Major strategy categories: (1) Long/Short Equity: long undervalued stocks, short overvalued stocks. Net long exposure (40-70%) means positive equity beta. Alpha from stock selection; beta from net exposure. Typical leverage: 1.5-3×. Best environments: stock-picker's markets with high cross-sectional dispersion. Poor environments: 2020 WallStreetBets short squeeze (GameStop), 2008 deleveraging; (2) Global Macro: directional bets on macro themes — currencies, interest rates, commodities, equity indices. High capacity, liquid markets. Discretionary macro (Soros, Druckenmiller) uses fundamental analysis; systematic macro (Man AHL, Winton) uses trend following models; (3) Relative Value / Stat Arb: exploit pricing discrepancies between related securities. Examples: convertible bond arbitrage, merger arbitrage (risk arb), fixed income relative value. Market-neutral — long the cheap, short the expensive; (4) Event-Driven: merger arbitrage (buying target post-announcement), activist investing, distressed debt (buying bonds of companies near bankruptcy). High idiosyncratic risk, uncorrelated with market except in credit crises; (5) Multi-Strategy: allocate across all strategies with daily risk management and frequent rebalancing. Examples: Citadel, Millennium, Point72. Diversified by design.

whenWhy.title: Hedge fund fee structures and incentives
whenWhy.body: Standard fee: 2% management fee (on AUM) + 20% incentive fee (on profits above hurdle rate). Impact: $100M fund earning 10% gross: Management fee = $2M; Incentive fee = $1.6M (20% of $8M net after management fee); Net return to investor ≈ 6.4%. For a 15% gross year: Investor nets ~10.4%; manager takes ~4.6%. High water mark (HWM): incentive fees only earned when NAV exceeds previous peak. Prevents double-charging fees after a drawdown and recovery. But creates zombie fund risk: if fund is far below HWM, manager has no near-term fee income and may take excessive risk or shut down. Hurdle rate: incentive fee only accrues above a minimum return (typically LIBOR/SOFR or 5%). Soft hurdle: incentive fee on all gains once hurdle is cleared. Hard hurdle: incentive fee only on gains above hurdle. Clawback: if incentive fees were paid early and later lost, investors claw back overpaid fees. Common in PE; rare in hedge funds. Emerging manager terms: often 1.5% / 15% to attract capital. Established managers (Renaissance Medallion): rumoured to charge 5% / 44%. Net return after fees matters most — IR net of fees, not gross.

whenWhy.title: Hedge fund due diligence
whenWhy.body: Investor due diligence framework: (1) Investment process: what is the manager's source of edge? Information advantage (alternative data, research depth)? Execution advantage (better algo, DMA)? Process advantage (disciplined risk management, systematic framework)? Can it be replicated by a cheaper instrument? (2) Track record: minimum 3-year, ideally 5+ year track record. Compute Sharpe ratio (net), IR against relevant benchmark, max drawdown, Calmar ratio. Adjust for backfill bias (funds only report after good performance), survivorship bias; (3) Risk management: who owns risk management? Independent from portfolio management? Limits on concentration, leverage, drawdown triggers; (4) Operational due diligence: auditor (Big 4 vs. unknown), fund administrator (independent?), prime broker (rehypothecation risk), legal structure (domicile, investor protections); (5) AUM and capacity: has the fund grown beyond its capacity? Crowding (many hedge funds in same position) reduces alpha and increases crash risk; (6) Key person risk: is the strategy dependent on one individual? What happens if they leave?

deepDiveIntro: Hedge fund return decomposition: R_HF = α + β_eq × R_equity + β_credit × R_credit_spread + β_vol × R_volatility + ε. Many 'hedge fund' strategies have significant equity beta (long/short equity: β ≈ 0.3-0.5) and credit beta (event-driven: β_credit ≈ 0.3). True alpha is the residual ε after stripping out all systematic risk exposures. Study (Fung & Hsieh 2004): 7-factor model explains most hedge fund returns — equity market, size spread, bond market, credit spread, currency trend, commodity trend, short-term rate trend. True alpha in most hedge funds ≈ 0-2%/year; not enough to justify 2+20 fees after costs.

deepDive.heading: Crowding and Systemic Risk in Hedge Funds
deepDive.body: Hedge fund crowding: when many hedge funds hold the same positions (e.g., long tech momentum, short value). Crowding analysis: measure overlap in 13F filings; compute 'hedge fund hotel stocks' (most commonly held). In normal markets: crowded trades work because many smart investors are in the same position. In crises: crowded trades amplify market dislocations. August 2007 'quant meltdown': multiple quant hedge funds simultaneously reduced leverage due to subprime losses in unrelated portfolios. Forced selling of their long positions and covering of shorts caused a reversal in common factor signals — momentum strategies lost 20%+ in 3 days. Mechanism: if one fund deleverages, it moves prices against others' positions, triggering their risk limits, causing more deleveraging — a fire sale spiral. Systemic risk: the hedge fund sector as a whole represents a significant fraction of financial market activity. Prime broker concentration (Goldman, Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan hold most hedge fund positions) means a prime broker failure (or prime broker risk-limit reduction) can trigger industry-wide deleveraging. Post-2008 reforms: central clearing of OTC derivatives, initial margin requirements — reduce but don't eliminate systemic risk. [1]

deepDive.heading: Replication and Liquid Alternatives
deepDive.body: Hedge fund replication: since Fung-Hsieh show hedge fund returns are largely explained by systematic factors, can you replicate hedge fund returns using liquid factor ETFs? Goldman Sachs Absolute Return Tracker (ART): replicates the HFRI composite using equity, bond, credit, currency, commodity factor exposures. Correlation with HFRI ≈ 0.7-0.8; cost ≈ 30-60bps vs. 2+20. The replication captures: (a) Most of the beta (systematic factor exposure); (b) Does NOT capture true alpha (manager skill); (c) Does NOT capture tail risk (e.g., short volatility strategies show good average returns but occasional crash — not captured by linear factor models). Liquid alternatives (liqalts): mutual funds or UCITS funds implementing hedge fund-like strategies with daily liquidity. Lower fees (0.7-1.5%). Constraints: no short selling beyond certain limits, leverage limits, liquidity requirements. Performance: liquid alts generally underperform their hedge fund equivalents after fees due to constraints. The illiquidity premium in hedge funds (long lock-up) is real: investors are compensated for accepting 1-3 year lock-up with higher expected returns. [2]

deepDive.heading: Hedge Fund Performance Persistence and Alpha
deepDive.body: Does past performance predict future performance? Evidence: (1) Short-term persistence (1-2 years): some evidence of persistence, particularly for short-term momentum in returns (Agarwal & Naik 2000); (2) Long-term persistence: mixed. Survival bias inflates measured persistence — bad funds close and disappear from databases; (3) Strategy-level persistence: trend-following CTAs show stronger persistence (structural factor) than discretionary long/short (individual skill); (4) Renaissance Medallion: the most famous case — consistent ~70% gross returns for 30+ years (employees only fund); attributed to superior signal research and execution. Extremely rare exception; (5) Average hedge fund alpha: studies (Fung & Hsieh 2004, Ibbotson et al. 2011): alpha is positive for the top quartile (2-4%/year), approximately zero for median, negative after fees for bottom quartile. The investor problem: identifying top-quartile funds in advance is very difficult. Persistence is weak enough that past winners are only slightly more likely to be future winners. Focus on process over track record: a rigorous, repeatable investment process with clear edge is more predictive than past returns. [1]

takeaways: Hedge fund strategies: L/S equity, global macro, relative value, event-driven, multi-strategy — each with distinct risk profile. [1]
Fee structure: 2+20; HWM prevents double-charging after drawdowns; net return (after fees) is what matters. [1]
Fung-Hsieh 7-factor model: explains most hedge fund returns; true alpha ≈ 0-2%/year after accounting for systematic risk. [2]
Crowding risk: many funds in same position amplifies crashes; August 2007 quant meltdown example. [1]
Alpha persistence: weak for average funds; stronger for systematic strategies; process quality > track record for prediction. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why do hedge fund databases show better performance than investors actually earn?
edgeCases.a: Multiple biases inflate reported hedge fund performance: (1) Survivorship bias: only funds that survived (and often performed well enough to continue reporting) remain in the database. Funds that closed due to poor performance are excluded. Impact: ~2-3%/year inflation in measured average returns; (2) Backfill bias (incubation bias): funds start reporting to databases after a period of good performance. The early (often good) track record is then backfilled into the database. Bad early periods that led to fund closures before database registration are never recorded. Impact: ~1.5%/year inflation; (3) Self-selection: funds with good recent performance are more likely to start reporting; funds doing badly stop reporting; (4) Stale pricing: illiquid hedge fund holdings (e.g., private credit, distressed debt) are reported at cost or last model value, not market value. This understates true volatility and overstates Sharpe ratios; (5) Performance smoothing: some managers systematically smooth reported returns over time (reducing apparent volatility). Getmansky, Lo & Makarov (2004) show many hedge funds have serial autocorrelation in returns inconsistent with truly liquid portfolios — evidence of smoothing or illiquidity. Combined effect: reported hedge fund alpha of 3-4%/year likely understates the case; but actual investor alpha (accounting for all biases and fees) is closer to 0-1%/year for the average investor.

edgeCases.q: What is the Kelly criterion and how does it apply to hedge fund sizing?
edgeCases.a: Kelly criterion: optimal fraction of wealth to bet = f* = (μ - r) / σ² (for continuous distributions), or f* = p - q (for binary bets where p = probability of win, q = probability of loss). Kelly maximises the expected log(wealth), which is equivalent to maximising long-run geometric growth rate. Application to hedge funds: for a portfolio with Sharpe ratio S = (μ - r) / σ: f* = S / σ = (μ - r) / σ². For S = 1, σ = 15%: f* = 1/0.15 ≈ 6.7 — meaning Kelly says invest 6.7× your wealth in the strategy (670% leverage!). In practice: (1) Full Kelly is too aggressive — it maximises expected log growth but leads to severe drawdowns (50% drawdowns are expected at Full Kelly); (2) Fractional Kelly (1/2 or 1/4 Kelly) is standard: half-Kelly gives 75% of the long-run return with much lower drawdown; (3) Kelly requires correct estimates of μ and σ — estimation error in μ can make Kelly severely over-bet; (4) Multi-asset Kelly: f* = Σ⁻¹ (μ - r) — the same formula as mean-variance optimal portfolio weights. Kelly and mean-variance are equivalent in continuous time with logarithmic utility. Hedge funds don't literally use Kelly, but the framework rationalises: why leveraging good strategies is optimal, why over-leveraging is dangerous, and why estimating edge correctly is critical.

sources.label: Fung, W. & Hsieh, D.A. (2004) — Hedge Fund Benchmarks: A Risk-Based Approach, FAJ
Agarwal, V. & Naik, N.Y. (2000) — Multi-Period Performance Persistence Analysis of Hedge Funds, JFQA
